concepts.title: Why gender & articles are the foundation of French
concepts.simpleExplanation: Every French noun is either masculine or feminine, and the little word in front of it (le/la, un/une, du/de la) shows which. Gender is mostly unpredictable, so the trick is to learn each noun together with its article, 'une table', not just 'table'. Get the article right and the rest of the sentence (adjectives, pronouns) falls into place.
concepts.analogy: It's like learning a person's name together with a photo: if you only memorise the name (the noun) without the face (the article), you'll recognise neither later.

rubricPoints.point: Every noun has a gender shown by its article
rubricPoints.reTeach: le/un = masculine, la/une = feminine; before a vowel both become l'.

rubricPoints.point: Learn the noun WITH its article
rubricPoints.reTeach: Store 'une maison', never bare 'maison', the article is part of the word.
du pain, de la viande, de l'eau; after a negation they become 'de'.

concepts.title: Nasal vowels & liaison: the French-accent secret
concepts.simpleExplanation: French has three vowels made by letting air out through your nose (on, an, in). Getting these right instantly makes you sound French. The other half of a French accent is liaison: a normally-silent final consonant gets pronounced and glued to the next word when that word starts with a vowel (vous_avez, les_amis).
concepts.analogy: Nasal vowels are like humming a vowel, the sound resonates in your nose. Liaison is like holding hands: a lonely silent consonant reaches out to grab the vowel of the next word.

rubricPoints.point: Three nasal vowels: on [o~], an [a~], in [e~]
rubricPoints.reTeach: Practise: bon, blanc, pain, let the sound go through your nose.
petit, grand, vous, the last consonant is usually silent.

rubricPoints.point: Liaison links silent consonant + next vowel
rubricPoints.reTeach: les amis -> [le-z-ami]; obligatory after articles and pronouns.

concepts.title: The present tense: one pattern rules them all
concepts.simpleExplanation: Regular -ER verbs are about 90% of all French verbs and share ONE ending pattern (-e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent). Learn it once and you can conjugate thousands of verbs. Add the two irregular giants être and avoir (plus aller and faire) and you can already talk about who you are, what you have, and what you do.
concepts.analogy: It's like a master key: -ER verbs all fit the same lock, so you cut one key (the endings) and it opens the whole building.

rubricPoints.reTeach: je parle, tu parles, nous parlons, the -ent ending is silent.

rubricPoints.point: être and avoir are irregular but essential
rubricPoints.reTeach: Memorise both fully; they also build the past tense later.
j'ai 20 ans, j'ai faim, j'ai froid, not 'je suis'.

concepts.title: Passé composé vs imparfait: the two pasts
concepts.simpleExplanation: French tells the past two ways. The passé composé reports a completed, punctual event ('what happened': j'ai mangé). The imparfait paints the background, ongoing states, habits and descriptions ('what was going on': je mangeais, il faisait beau, quand j'étais petit). Choosing the right one is what makes past narration sound natural.
concepts.analogy: Think of a film: the imparfait is the set and the weather (the backdrop that was already there), the passé composé is each action the actors perform on top of it.

rubricPoints.reTeach: avoir/être + participle: j'ai fini, elle est partie.
Stem from 'nous' + -ais/-ait/-ions...: je faisais, il pleuvait.
Il faisait beau (background) quand je suis sorti (event).

concepts.title: Object pronouns + y and en
concepts.simpleExplanation: Pronouns replace nouns so you don't repeat them, and in French they go BEFORE the verb. Direct objects use le/la/les, indirect (people after 'à') use lui/leur. Two special ones: y replaces a place or 'à + thing' (j'y vais), and en replaces 'de + thing' or a quantity (j'en ai trois).
concepts.analogy: Pronouns are like pointing instead of naming: once everyone knows what you mean, you just point ('la', 'lui', 'y') rather than saying the whole thing again.

rubricPoints.reTeach: Je vois Marie -> je la vois; je parle à Marie -> je lui parle.
Je vais à Paris -> j'y vais.
Tu veux du café ? -> Oui, j'en veux.

concepts.title: The subjunctive: the mood of subjectivity
concepts.simpleExplanation: The subjonctif appears after 'que' when the main clause expresses subjectivity, necessity, wish, emotion or doubt (il faut que, je veux que, je suis content que, bien que). It's formed from the 'ils' stem + -e endings, with a few key irregulars (sois, aie, aille, fasse, puisse). It's a B1 milestone and a DELF favourite.
concepts.analogy: Indicative states facts ('what is'); the subjunctive lives in the world of wishes and maybes ('what should/might be'), like the difference between a news report and a wish list.

rubricPoints.reTeach: il faut que, vouloir que, être content que, bien que.
ils parlent -> que je parle; endings -e -es -e -ions -iez -ent.
être->sois, avoir->aie, aller->aille, faire->fasse, pouvoir->puisse.

concepts.title: The conditional & si-clauses
concepts.simpleExplanation: The conditionnel (future stem + imparfait endings) is for politeness, wishes and hypotheses (je voudrais, il faudrait). Its home is the hypothesis pattern: Si + imparfait, conditionnel présent, 'Si j'avais le temps, je voyagerais' (If I had time, I would travel). At B2 this extends to the unreal past: Si + plus-que-parfait, conditionnel passé.
concepts.analogy: It's the 'what if' tense: it steps out of reality into an imagined world where things would be different if a condition were met.

rubricPoints.reTeach: je voudrais, tu aimerais, il faudrait.
Si j'avais le temps, je viendrais.
Je voudrais un café; pourriez-vous m'aider ?

concepts.title: Articulators: structuring an argument
concepts.simpleExplanation: At B2 the DELF wants a structured, argued position. The tools are logical connectors: introduce (tout d'abord), add (par ailleurs, de plus), contrast (en revanche, néanmoins, cependant), illustrate (en effet, par exemple), and conclude (en somme, par conséquent). Sprinkling these correctly is the difference between a list of sentences and a real argument.
concepts.analogy: Connectors are the signposts on a road: without them the reader gets lost; with them, they always know whether you're adding, objecting, giving an example, or arriving.

rubricPoints.reTeach: tout d'abord, d'une part / d'autre part, par ailleurs, de plus.
en revanche, néanmoins, toutefois, cependant, certes... mais.
en effet, par exemple, ainsi; en somme, par conséquent, en définitive.

concepts.title: How the DELF exam works
concepts.simpleExplanation: The DELF (B1 and B2) has four sections, each scored out of 25: compréhension de l'oral (listening), compréhension des écrits (reading), production écrite (writing), production orale (speaking). To pass you need at least 50/100 total AND at least 5/25 in every single section, score below 5 anywhere and you fail, however high the rest. Knowing the format and the pass rule is half the battle.
concepts.analogy: It's like a four-legged table: the total has to be high enough, but every leg must also be long enough on its own, one collapsed leg (a section under 5) topples the whole thing.

rubricPoints.reTeach: Listening, reading, writing (≈160 words B1 / 250 words B2), speaking.

rubricPoints.point: Pass rule: total ≥ 50/100 AND ≥ 5/25 per section
rubricPoints.reTeach: A section below 5/25 is disqualifying, whatever the total.
The mock exams score each section and apply the real DELF pass rule.
